coredata.dc:title: Metal-organic-framework-confined quantum dots enhance photocurrent signals: A molecularly imprinted photoelectrochemical cathodic sensor for rapid and sensitive tetracycline detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tao
Zhang, Mengge
Lu, Yuhao
Liu, Qian
Niu, Qijian
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description:
Background Tetracycline (TC), a cost-effective broad-spectrum antibacterial drug, has been excessively utilized in the livestock and poultry industry, leading to a serious overabundance of TC in livestock wastewater. However, conventional analytical methods such as liquid chromatography and gas chromatography face challenges in achieving sensitive detection of trace amounts of TC in complex substrates. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a highly sensitive and anti-interference analytical method for the detection of tetracycline in livestock wastewater.
Results A porphyrin-based MOF (PCN-224)-confined carbon dots (CDs) material (CDs@PCN-224) was synthesized by a “bottle-around-ship” strategy. The reduced carrier migration distance is conducive to the separation of electron-hole pairs and enhanced the photocurrent signal due to the tight coupling of CDs and PCN-224. Further, molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was synthesized by rapid in-situ UV-polymerization and employed as a recognition element. The specific recognition of the target by imprinted cavities blocks electron transfer, resulting in a “turn off” response signal, thus realizing the selective detection of TC. Under optimal conditions, the constructed MIP-PEC cathodic sensor detected 1.00 × 10−12 M to 1.00 × 10−7 M of TC sensitively, with a limit of detection of 3.72 × 10−13 M. In addition, the proposed MIP-PEC sensor demonstrated good TC detection performance in actual livestock wastewater.
Significance The strategy based on MOF pore-confined quantum dots can effectively enhance the photocurrent response of the photosensitive substrate. Simultaneously, the MIP constructed by in-situ rapid UV-polymerization showed excellent anti-interference and reusable properties. This work provides a promising MIP-PEC cathodic sensing method for the rapid and sensitive detection of antibiotics in complex-matrix environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical cathodic sensor
Metal-organic frameworks
Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
UV-Polymerization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Description of CDs, PCN-224 and CD@PCN-224
PEC performance and enhancement mechanism of CDs@PCN-224
Analytical performance of the MIP-PEC sensor in TC recognition
Selectivity, reproducibility, stability and reusability of the MIP-PEC sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 62101216 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20200914 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2022T150275 , 2020M671359 ), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions ( PAPD-2018-87 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CDs (A). SEM images of PCN-224 (B) and CDs@PCN-224 (C). HRTEM image of CD@PCN-224 (D). (E) Enlargement of the yellow box in (D). Lattice fringes of CD@PCN-224 under HRTEM (F). XRD patterns of CDs, PCN-224 and CDs@PCN-224 (G). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms curves of PCN-224 and CD@PCN-224 (H; inset: pore diameter distribution) and zeta potentials of CDs, PCN-224 and CDs@PCN-224 (I). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photocurrent signals of PCN-224 and CDs@PCN-224 (A). UV–Vis DRS spectra of PCN-224 and CDs@PCN-224 (B). FL spectra for CDs, PCN-224 and CDs@PCN-224 (excited at 350 nm) (C) and schematic illustration of the electron-transfer process (D).

Photocurrent responses of CDs@PCN-224/ITO, NIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO and r-MIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO before and after immersion in 1.00 × 10−8 M TC (A). Photocurrent responses (B) of ITO (curve a), CDs@PCN-224/ITO (curve b), MIPs/CDs@PCN-224/ITO (curve c), r-MIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO (curve d) and after incubation in 1.00 × 10−9 M (curve e) and 1.00 × 10−8 M TC (curve f). Nyquist plots (C) and cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves (D) of ITO (curve a), CD@PCN-224/ITO (curve b) and r-MIP/CD@PCN-224/ITO (curve c), and after incubation in 1.00 × 10−8 M TC solution (curve d).

Relationships between the photocurrent response of the MIP-PEC sensor and the concentration of CD@PCN-224 modified on ITO (A), the molar ratio of template molecule to functional monomer (B), the MIP precursor solution addition volume (C), elution time (D), incubation time (E) and electrolyte pH (F).

Photocurrent responses of the MIP-PEC sensor to different TC concentrations (from a to k: 1.00 × 10−12, 5.00 × 10−11, 1.00 × 10−11, 5.00 × 10−10, 1.00 × 10−10, 5.00 × 10−9,1.00 × 10−9, 5.00 × 10−8, 1.00 × 10−8, 5.00 × 10−7 and 1.00 × 10−7 M) (A). Corresponding linear calibration curve (n = 3) for TC detection (B). By selectivity (C), reproducibility (D), time-based photocurrent response (E) and reusability (F) of the developed MIP-PEC sensor.
Fabrication procedure of the MIP-PEC sensor based on CD@PCN-224 for TC detection.
TC detection in livestock wastewater according to the developed MIP-PEC sensor and LC-MS/MS.

head.ce:title: Metal-organic-framework-confined quantum dots enhance photocurrent signals: A molecularly imprinted photoelectrochemical cathodic sensor for rapid and sensitive tetracycline detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC), a cost-effective broad-spectrum antibacterial drug, has been excessively utilized in the livestock and poultry industry, leading to a serious overabundance of TC in livestock wastewater. However, conventional analytical methods such as liquid chromatography and gas chromatography face challenges in achieving sensitive detection of trace amounts of TC in complex substrates. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a highly sensitive and anti-interference analytical method for the detection of tetracycline in livestock wastewater.

A porphyrin-based MOF (PCN-224)-confined carbon dots (CDs) material (CDs@PCN-224) was synthesized by a “bottle-around-ship” strategy. The reduced carrier migration distance is conducive to the separation of electron-hole pairs and enhanced the photocurrent signal due to the tight coupling of CDs and PCN-224. Further, molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was synthesized by rapid in-situ UV-polymerization and employed as a recognition element. The specific recognition of the target by imprinted cavities blocks electron transfer, resulting in a “turn off” response signal, thus realizing the selective detection of TC. Under optimal conditions, the constructed MIP-PEC cathodic sensor detected 1.00 × 10−12 M to 1.00 × 10−7 M of TC sensitively, with a limit of detection of 3.72 × 10−13 M. In addition, the proposed MIP-PEC sensor demonstrated good TC detection performance in actual livestock wastewater.

The strategy based on MOF pore-confined quantum dots can effectively enhance the photocurrent response of the photosensitive substrate. Simultaneously, the MIP constructed by in-situ rapid UV-polymerization showed excellent anti-interference and reusable properties. This work provides a promising MIP-PEC cathodic sensing method for the rapid and sensitive detection of antibiotics in complex-matrix environmental samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is a low-cost drug with excellent broad-spectrum antibiotic performance, which is frequently used in the livestock industry to treat infectious diseases and promote growth [1,2]. However, only about 15 % of the dosage is effectively absorbed by organismal tissues, with the remainder released into the surrounding environment [3]. This can contaminate meat products and drinking water, eventually threatening human health via the food chain [4]. Therefore, it is necessary to detect TC in livestock wastewater to protect the environment and human health. Various analytical methods have been applied to quantify TC concentrations. Conventional techniques, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC-UV) [5], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectroscopy (LC-MS/MS) [6] and capillary electrophoresis (CE-UV) [7] have high accuracy and sensitivity. However, these methods require expensive equipment and specialized laboratory procedures. Accordingly, advanced analytical methods with low cost and easy operation have been developed for TC detection. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing is a promising, novel technique that has attracted extensive attention for its simple apparatus, rapid analysis and high sensitivity [8,9].

The PEC sensors developed for the selective detection of target mainly use recognition elements based on aptamers [10,11], antigenic-antibodies [12] and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [13]. Known as “artificial antibodies”, MIPs exhibit high chemical stability, low cost and a simple preparation process [14]. For example, Sheng et al. developed a MIP-PEC sensor based on a metal-organic framework (MOF) derived TiO2 with recognition sites by a simple mixed-solvothermal and calcination method for the detection of TC [15]. Photosensitive materials derived from MOFs provide a large specific surface area, which can improve the adsorption and recognition of TC when combined with MIP. In addition, a series of MIP-PEC sensors constructed by other polymerization methods have shown good analytical performance in other antibiotic detection applications [16]. Notably, among the various polymerization methods, ultraviolet-initiated polymerization (UV-polymerization) is considered an ideal approach for MIPs construction as it features rapid polymerization and in-situ controllable preparation, and requires mild reaction conditions [17].

Although MIP-PEC sensors show excellent selectivity, the introduction of MIPs generally decreases the photocurrent response of photosensitive materials [18]. Meanwhile, in contrast to photoanodic materials, cathodic photosensitive materials can effectively inhibit intrinsic hole-oxidation reactions and avoid interference caused by the reducing substances found in complex environmental samples. Therefore, it is desirable to develop photosensitive materials that have stronger cathodic photocurrent signals. A novel porphyrin-based MOF photosensitive material, PCN-224, exhibits a stable cathodic photocurrent signal and has the advantages of a large surface area, high porosity, tuneable pores and easy functionalization [19,20]. Nevertheless, the photocurrent response of PCN-224 is limited by the rapid reorganization of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [21]. To overcome this defect, an effective strategy is to construct heterojunctions via coupling with other photosensitive materials, such as PCN-224(Cu)/TiO2 [22], CdS@PCN-224 [23], C60@PCN-224 [24] or PCN-224/rGO [25]. Compared to metal-containing semiconductor materials, carbon-based nanomaterials have high light absorption, delocalized conjugated structures, and superior electron-transport ability. Carbon dots (CDs) is a novel material with size controllability and easy functionalization that has recently been reported as able to couple with PCN-224 to form a promising material for photodynamic therapy. Using electrostatic adsorption, CDs can be coupled to PCN-224 to enhance light utilization and energy transfer efficiency [26]. Therefore, considering the matched sizes and coordination interactions between CDs and PCN-224, a predictably enhanced photocurrent signal could be acquired by encapsulating CDs into the pores of PCN-224 directly via in-situ synthesis method.

Herein, a novel MOF pore-confined quantum dots photosensitive material was designed and synthesized to construct a novel MIP-PEC cathodic sensor for rapid and sensitive TC detection (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, an amplified cathodic photocurrent signal was acquired by encapsulating CDs into the pores of PCN-224, which reduces the carrier migration distance and promotes electron-hole pair separation. Then, UV-polymerization method was used for the rapid and in-situ fabrication of MIP film that can detect TC selectively and improve the stabilities of electrode interface simultaneously. Finally, a calibration equation was derived from the relationship between photocurrent variation (ΔI) and the logarithm of TC concentration under optimal conditions. This novel system was then applied to the analysis of real livestock wastewater.
A complete list of the materials, reagents and instruments used in this study is presented in the Supplementary Information.

Firstly, CDs was integrated based on the literature [27], as described in the Supplementary Information. Afterwards, ZrOCl2·8H2O (300 mg), H2TCPP (100 mg) and benzoic acid (2.8 g) were dissolved in 100 mL DMF. Next, a certain volume of CDs solution was quickly added into the above solution and stirred for 10 min. After that, the mixture was transferred into a 250 mL round-bottom flask and stirred at 90 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, CD@PCN-224 nanoparticles were collected after centrifugation (13,000 rpm, 30 min), followed by washing with DMF three times and acetone twice. Finally, the precipitated CD@PCN-224 nanoparticles were dried in a vacuum dryer to acquire a dark purple powder. The whole material synthesis process is shown in Fig. S1. For comparison, pure PCN-224 nanoparticles were prepared by the above method without the addition of CDs.

Bare indium-tin oxide (ITO) slices (with a modified area of 0.2826 cm2) were boiled in 1 M NaOH solution, ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and ultra-pure water for 15 min, then dried at 37 °C [28,29]. Subsequently, the treated ITO slices were coated with 20 μL of CD@PCN-224 suspension and air-dried under sheltered conditions for further modification of the MIP.

The MIP was prepared according to the literature [30] with minor modifications. A MIP precursor solution containing TC, MAA (340 μL), EDGMA (291 μL) and AIBN (82.1 mg; MAA:EGDMA:AIBN = 8:3:1, n/n) was dropped onto the CDs@PCN-224 modified electrodes. The TC was used as the template molecule, while MAA and EGDMA acted as the functional monomers and cross-linkers, respectively, and AIBN served as the photo-initiator. After polymerizing under 365 nm UV light for 2 min with nitrogen protection, MIPs/CD@PCN-224/ITO electrode was acquired before the elution of template molecule. As a control, non-imprinted polymer modified CDs@PCN-224/ITO (NIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO) was produced by the same method without TC addition. Finally, r-MIP/CD@PCN-224/ITO was obtained after eluting the template molecule in methanol-acetic acid (9:1; v/v) for a certain time.

For TC analysis, the original r-MIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO signal was first measured and denoted as I 0. Then, r-MIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO was immersed in a TC solution. Subsequently, the electrodes were removed and cleaned with purified water to remove non-specifically adsorbed substances, and the response signal was denoted as I 1. The photocurrent variation (ΔI = I 1 – I 0) was used for the quantification of TC. All photocurrent signals were measured in 0.01 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with a Xe lamp turned on and off at 20 s intervals. And the applied bias voltage was 0V (vs. Ag/AgCl) [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Description of CDs, PCN-224 and CD@PCN-224
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The CDs was quasi-spherical particles with a mean size of around 2.7 nm (Fig. 1 A). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S2A) showed a peak centred at 340 nm, while the fluorescence (FL) spectrum (Fig. S2B) demonstrated an emission peak at around 440 nm. This may be due to the doping of amino groups (-NH2) on the surface of the CDs (Fig. S2C), which could effectively reduce the non-radiative transition [33]. Moreover, as displayed in Fig. S2D, the emission peak did not shift under different excitation wavelengths, indicating that the CDs was of uniform size and had a stable surface composition [23].

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of PCN-224 (Fig. 1B) shows relatively uniform spherical particles with an average size of about 100 nm, whereas the CDs@PCN-224 showed irregular spherical shapes and a slightly larger particle size of approximately 130 nm (Fig. 1C). This may due to the abundant –NH2 groups on the CDs bonding with the carboxyl groups (-COOH) of H2TCPP, which consequently affected the growth of MOF particles. Additionally, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was utilized to discover the microscopic structure of CDs@PCN-224 (Fig. 1D). This found a distinct presence of CDs (Fig. 1E) and a 0.34 nm lattice gap (Fig. 1F) corresponding to the (002) crystal plane [34].

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were also obtained to investigate the crystalline structure of the materials. As shown in Fig. 1G, PCN-224 and CDs@PCN-224 showed the same diffraction peaks at 2θ = 6.54°, 7.90°, 9.12° and 11.14°, which represent the (022), (222), (004) and (224) crystal planes, respectively [35]. In addition, a reduction in the intensity of the above peaks and a slight enhancement at 21.5° can be attributed to the successful confinement of CDs [36]. Meanwhile, XPS (Fig. S3), EDS and element mapping scanning (Fig. S4) were used to elucidate the composition of CDs@PCN-224, which indicated the presence of C, N, O and Zr elements. Compared to the available literature [37], the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. S3B) shows a new peak at 288.6 eV corresponding to the N–CO bond [37], which confirms that cooperative bonding occurred between CDs and H2TCPP during the preparation of CD@PCN-224.

Next, N2 adsorption-desorption experiments were conducted to prove that CDs were encapsulated into the pores of PCN-224. Compared with the original PCN-224, the specific surface area and pore volume of the CDs@PCN-224 material were significantly reduced (Fig. 1H and Table S1). This is because the encapsulation of CDs by MOF led to the closure of its pore structure [38]. The zeta potentials of PCN-224, CDs and CDs@PCN-224 were characterized as +22.1, −23.4 and + 21.2 mV, respectively (Fig. 1I). The combination with CDs had little influence on the zeta potential of PCN-224, which means most of the CDs were confined in its pores rather than adsorbed on its surface. These characterizations demonstrate the successful synthesis of CDs@PCN-224.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC performance and enhancement mechanism of CDs@PCN-224
ce:section.ce:para: The photocurrent responses of PCN-224 and CD@PCN-224 were measured. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, PCN-224 had a low cathodic photocurrent response of −300 nA. An enhanced photocurrent signal was obtained for CDs@PCN-224, which was approximately a 3.0-fold increase compared to PCN-224. This can be attributed to the sensitization effect of CDs, which caused efficient separation of electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the fact that no fluorescence of CDs appeared in the electrolyte after acquiring a photocurrent signal using CD@PCN-224/ITO as working electrodes proves the tight coupling of CDs and PCN-224. This solves the problem of electrode interface instability caused by the excellent aqueous dispersibility of CDs (Fig. S5) [39].

The mechanism for the enhanced PEC signal was explored by optical and electrochemical methods. Firstly, the light-absorption capacities of PCN-224 and CD@PCN-224 were investigated by obtaining their UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS), as displayed in Fig. 2B. CDs@PCN-224 showed enhanced absorption capacities in the UV–Vis region, while its absorption edge extended to 800 nm due to the sensitization effect of CDs.

In addition, the energy levels of CDs and PCN-224 were explored to study the electron-transfer process. Firstly, CDs was estimated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). The initial reduction potential (Fig. S6A) and oxidation potential (Fig. S6B) were −1.10 V and 1.26 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively, which correspond to the highest occupied molecular orbital (E HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (E LUMO) values of CDs [40]. The Ag/AgCl potential is shifted to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale based on the Nernst equation [41]:
(1)
E RHE (V) = E Ag/AgCl + E 0 Ag/AgCl + 0.059 pH
Where E0 Ag/AgCl is the electrode potential of the Ag/AgCl reference electrode, E 0 Ag/AgCl = 0.1976 V at 25 °C. The pH refers to the pH value of electrolyte (0.5 M Na2SO4), pH = 7.0 at 25 °C. E Ag/AgCl is the experimentally measured potential on the Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and E RHE is the conversion potential relative to RHE. Finally, the E HOMO and E LUMO of the CDs were calculated to be −0.49 V and 1.87 V (vs. RHE), respectively.

Meanwhile, the flat-band potential (E FB) of PCN-224 was calculated using the Mott-Schottky measurement method (Fig. S7A). The slope of PCN-224 exhibited typical n-type semiconductor characteristics. The E FB of n-type semiconductors is almost the same as its conduction band edge (E CB) [42]; thus, the E CB of PCN-224 was estimated to be +0.09 V (vs. RHE). With the bandgap of PCN-224 measured to be 1.80 eV based on its Tauc's-plot (Fig. S7B) [43], its valence band edge potential (E VB) was calculated to be +1.89 eV (vs. RHE).

According to the above results, CDs and PCN-224 exhibited a matching energy band that accelerated the separation of electron-hole pairs, which was verified by the fluorescence signal of the materials. Usually, the faster recombination rate corresponds to a stronger fluorescence signal [18,44,45]. As shown in Fig. 2C, the CD@PCN-224 exhibited coexistent emission peaks of pristine CDs (440 nm) and PCN-224 (660 and 720 nm). The fluorescence intensity attributed to PCN-224 showed a significant reduction, which is related to the effective separation of electron-hole pairs.

The electron-transfer process shown in Fig. 2D indicates that a type-Ⅱ heterojunction was constructed in CD@PCN-224. Under light excitation, the photoelectrons on the LUMO of the CDs were transferred to the CB of PCN-224 further to the electrolyte. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes transferred from the VB of PCN-224 to the HOMO of CDs, and were finally consumed by the electrons from ITO. Accordingly, the photoexcited electron-hole pairs could be separated efficiently, resulting in an amplified cathodic photocurrent signal for CDs@PCN-224.

The introduction of MIP was characterized by SEM images of the electrode interfaces. As displayed in Fig. S8, CD@PCN-224/ITO, MIPs/CD@PCN-224/ITO and r-MIP/CD@PCN-224/ITO were observed sequentially. Firstly, CDs@PCN-224 was uniformly modified on the ITO electrode. After UV-polymerization, it was clear that the electrode interface was covered with polymer film, as shown in Fig. S8B. Finally, imprinted cavities were formed after eluting the template molecule, as shown in Fig. S8C, indicating that the MIP-PEC sensor was constructed.

Furthermore, to prove the imprinting effect of the fabricated MIP-PEC sensor, the photocurrent responses of CDs@PCN-224/ITO, NIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO and r-MIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO were recorded before and after incubation in a 1.00 × 10−8 M TC solution. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, for CDs@PCN-224/ITO and NIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO, there was little change in photocurrent after incubation with TC. Only r-MIP/CD@PCN-224/ITO exhibited a significant photocurrent variation, proving that MIP film had a particular identification ability.

Subsequently, the photocurrent signal responses of different electrodes further proved the feasibility of the MIP-PEC sensor. As shown in Fig. 3B, the bare ITO electrode showed nearly no photocurrent reaction (curve a), while the CDs@PCN-224/ITO (curve b) electrode exhibited a greatly enhanced cathodic photocurrent response (−820 nA). The r-MIP/CD@PCN-224/ITO (curve d) showed a stronger intensity (−750 nA) compared to MIPs/CD@PCN-224/ITO (curve c). After incubation with 1.00 × 10−9 (curve e) and 1.00 × 10−8 M (curve f) TC, with increases in TC concentration, the photocurrent decreased significantly, which is because of the specific identification of MIP. TC was successfully captured by the imprinted cavities, which greatly hindered the electron transfer. Meanwhile, the introduction of the polymer film further enhanced the stability of the electrode interface and weakened the photocurrent attenuation caused by the redox reaction at the electrode interface (Fig. S9).

To demonstrate the feasibility of the MIP-PEC sensor for TC detection, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) trials were implemented in a potassium ferricyanide/potassium ferrocyanide (K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6) redox-couple system to evaluate the electrochemical behaviour of the electrode interface. The diameter of the semicircle at high frequencies of EIS was assigned to the electron-transfer resistance (R et) [46]. As shown in Fig. 3C, the R et of the CDs@PCN-224/ITO electrode was low (curve b), indicating that CD@PCN-224/ITO has a great electron transport capability. However, after modifying the MIP film on the electrode, the resistance of the electrode increased significantly due to the weak conductivity of the polymer film (curve c) [17]. With further incubation with TC, there was a significant increase in resistance. This suggests that the target recognition of the MIP-PEC sensor is due to the steric hindrance effect resulting from its binding to the imprinted cavities. Additionally, the peak current and peak potential difference (Δ Ep) of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- were calculated by CV. This showed that electron diffusion between the electrolyte and electrode interface of diverse modified electrodes. As displayed in Fig. 3D, the peak current of CD@PCN-224/ITO was about 1.2 mA (curve b); however, after MIP was modified on the electrode, its peak current decreased to 0.9 mA (curve c). After incubation with TC, there was a significant decrease in peak current to 0.6 mA with the highest value of ΔEp (curve d) on account of reoccupation of the imprinted cavities. The CV results are consistent with those of EIS. Both demonstrate the feasibility of the sensor for TC detection and that the detection mechanism is reduced electron transfer ability caused by steric hindrance.

To achieve the best analytical performance of the MIP-PEC sensor, the experimental conditions were optimized. Firstly, the amounts of CDs addition in CDs@PCN-224 were varied. As shown in Fig. S10A, when the volume of CDs solution was 20 μL, which is equivalent to a mass ratio of 1.2 wt% in the composite, the photocurrent reached its maximum value of about −600 nA. Subsequently, the photocurrent signal gradually decreased with further addition of CDs. This is because excessive CDs can block the channels of PCN-224, resulting in a slow migration rate of photogenerated carriers. Next, the modification concentration of CDs@PCN-224 was optimized. As shown in Fig. 4 A, when the concentration reached 2 mg·mL−1, the photocurrent response remained almost stable at −800 nA, considering that the utilization of light had reached saturation. Accordingly, the volume of added CDs and the modification concentration of CD@PCN-224 were set to 20 μL and 3 mg·mL−1, respectively, for the following experiments.

In addition, the strength at which the imprinted cavities are integrated with the template is relative to the molar ratio of the templates to the functional monomer. Fig. 4B shows that the maximum ΔI occurred at 1.5 %. This is because when the amount of functional monomer is low, the adsorption capacity of the imprinting cavity decreases. However, a high concentration of functional monomers can lead to concurrent forms of MIP and NIP, thus reducing the specific recognition of template molecules [47]. Hence, the best molar rate was considered to be 1.5 %. In addition, Fig. 4C shows the optimization of the volume of the MIP precursor solution added on the electrode interface. The volume of precursor led to changes in the thickness of the MIP film [48]. On the one hand, the thickness of the film affected the photocurrent response before the target recognition (I 0); on the other hand, it affected the stability of the electrode interface, and further affect the photocurrent variation (ΔI). Therefore, the current responses of different addition volumes before and after target identification were recorded in Fig. S10B. I0 gradually decreased with the increase of film thickness, which was due to the weak conductivity of the MIP hindered the electron transfer [49]. The photocurrent response after identifying the same concentration of the target (I 1) increased first and then decreased, and reached the maximum value at 2.5 μL. This is because the thinner molecular imprinting film has less inhibition on photocurrent, and compared with 2.0 μL, the addition volume of 2.5 μL provides more stable recognition sites for the molecular imprinting layer [50], so that the detection of TC in a wider concentration range can be realized. Therefore, 2.5 μL was chosen as the optimal addition volume.

In the MIP-PEC sensor, the template elution time directly affects the surface properties of the MIP film. As shown in Fig. 4D, the photocurrent reaction of r-MIP/CDs@PCN-224/ITO gradually increased from 5 min to 15 min and stabilized at 15 min. Hence, a 20 min elution time was chosen to ensure complete elution of the template monomer. Subsequently, as shown in Fig. 4E, ΔI gradually increased with incubation time from 5 min to 15 min and remained essentially unchanged after 15 min. This is due to the MIP membrane reaching its maximum TC adsorption ability. Thus, a 20 min incubation time was chosen for subsequent experiments.

Finally, Fig. 4F exhibits the influence of the electrolyte pH on the response signal of the MIP-PEC sensor. The results show that ΔI was highest at pH 7.4, which may be due to TC having better structural stability at pH 7.4, leading to the imprinted cavities having higher target recognition efficiency [51]. Thus, pH 7.4 was chosen for use in this experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the MIP-PEC sensor in TC recognition
ce:section.ce:para: Various concentrations of TC were detected by the constructed MIP-PEC sensor under the optimized conditions described above. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the photocurrent gradually decreased with higher concentrations. There was an excellent linear relationship between ΔI and the logarithm of TC concentration within the range of 1.00 × 10−12 - 1.00 × 10−7 M (Fig. 5B). The calibration equation (n = 3) was ΔI = 131.786 lg C TC + 1628.481 (R 2 = 0.997). The limit of detection (LOD) of the MIP-PEC sensor is calculated to be 3.72 × 10−13 M using the International Union for Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) recommended method [52]. The details to calculate the value is recorded in Supplementary Information. In addition, compared with other reported MIP-based sensors (Table S2), the MIP-PEC sensor exhibited a satisfying linearity range and a lower LOD for TC detection. The reason for the excellent performance is that the PEC sensor has the intrinsic advantage of low background signal. Further, in this work, the enhanced cathodic photocurrent and the large specific surface area of CDs@PCN-224 are the important reasons for the excellent performance of the MIP-PEC sensor, thus exhibiting a wider detection range and lower limit of detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, stability and reusability of the MIP-PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the MIP-PEC sensor, antibiotics which have similar chemical structure to TC such as oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlorotetracycline (CTC), and antibiotics which might coexist with TC in the actual livestock wastewater including roxithromycin (RM), ofloxacin (OFL) and sulfadimidine (SA) were used as interfering substances to record the photocurrent responds. The concentrations of these interfering antibiotics were 10 times higher than TC, resulting in negligible ΔI (Fig. 5C). Only in the presence of TC did ΔI have a significant response, which means that the established MIP-PEC sensor has excellent selectivity [53].

To assess the repeatability of the MIP-PEC sensor, the photocurrents of six parallel electrodes fabricated under identical conditions were acquired. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 1.9 % (Fig. 5D), demonstrating good reproducibility. Fig. 5E shows the photocurrent response for 10 repeated off-on-off operations, which has no obvious change. Moreover, the MIP-PEC sensor exhibited great reusability and the recovery time of the working electrode included three processes of elution, incubation and detection, with a total of 43 min. As shown in Fig. 5F, when the same electrode was used for five consecutive TC detections, ΔI remained at 86.1 % of the first response value. In addition, the 7-day stability was investigated, with no significant decay in photocurrent response observed and an RSD of 2.5 % (Fig. S11). This reveals the satisfactory stability of the MIP-PEC sensor.

To evaluate its application, the developed MIP-PEC sensor was applied to analysis the actual livestock wastewater from ditches around the Muyuan farming of Zhenjiang, which may contain TC. The sample was first filtered to remove suspended solids and then analysed using the MIP-PEC sensor. As shown in Table 1 , no TC was detected in the wastewater, and the result was verified by LC-MS/MS. Then, to eliminate interference from the complex matrix in the actual wastewater, a standard curve of the sensor in actual sample detection was established using the standard addition method. As shown in Fig. S12A, the photocurrent signals decreased as the TC concentration increased. A good linear relationship between the logarithm of TC concentration and ΔI was established (Fig. S12B), with a linear relationship of ΔI = 97.769 lg C TC + 1344.019 (R 2 = 0.995). However, the linear range (1.00 × 10−11 - 1.00 × 10−8 M) and sensitivity of the sensor when used in actual wastewater samples were lower compared to the standard curve in PBS due to the effect of the matrix in the actual sample [54].

In addition, the application performance of the sensor was estimated utilizing the spiking and recovery method. The recovery rate of the MIP-PEC sensor was 89–97 %, with an RSD of 1.72–4.07 % (Table 1). In order to verify the reliability of the proposed method, WEIPU Testing Technology Service (Shanghai) was commissioned to measure TC using Triple Quad LC-MS/MS 1290 Infinity II/6460 (DB 37/T 3632-2019). The results are consistent with those of the developed MIP-PEC sensor, demonstrating that the established method has great accuracy in actual applications.

This study fabricated a material consisting of PCN-224 with pore-confined CDs to amplify a cathodic photocurrent signal. The tight coupling of CDs and PCN-224 enhances light utilization and accelerates the separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Then, a UV-polymerization method was adopted for the rapid and in-situ construction of the MIP-PEC sensor. This sensor displayed excellent analytical performance in TC detection and acceptable selectivity, reproducibility, stability and reusability. Meanwhile, the MIP-PEC sensor was successfully applied to TC detection in a real livestock wastewater sample, with the results consistent with those of LC-MS/MS. Therefore, the developed signal amplification strategy and MIP-PEC sensor hold great promise for the monitoring of antibiotics and other organic pollutants in complex environmental samples.

Tao Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Mengge Zhang: Software, Resources. Yuhao Lu: Software, Resources. Qian Liu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Qijian Niu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Tianyan You: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62101216), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20200914), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022T150275, 2020M671359), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD-2018-87).

ce:section.ce:para:
